hypotheses.label: GLTF+AnimationMixer+IBL Rendering Stack
hypotheses.researchQuestion: q-pr-01 / q-pr-02 — Rendering approach and character visual pipeline
hypotheses.statement: This stack is optimal for this D/L/A structure because: GLTF (DATA) provides schema-validated PBR material definitions and skeletal animation data; AnimationMixer (LOGIC) maps AnimationClip[] to state-driven crossfade decisions; IBL via PMREMGenerator (LOGIC) derives environment lighting without per-frame light computation; SkeletonUtils.clone() (ACTION) instantiates multiple independent spacekid entities with correct bone isolation. The ontology surface maps cleanly: SpacekidEntity = DATA struct, AnimationState = LOGIC reducer, CharacterSpawn = ACTION type.
hypotheses.dlaJustification: DATA: SpacekidConfig (GLTF asset path, material overrides, skeleton topology), SpaceshipConfig (procedural geometry params, PBR material params). LOGIC: AnimationStateMachine (crossfade decisions per interaction mode), PBRLightingModel (IBL + MeshPhysicalMaterial parameter derivation), LODSelector (camera-distance to mesh-level mapping). ACTION: CharacterSpawn (SkeletonUtils.clone + scene.add), AnimationTransition (crossFadeTo), MaterialApply (assign MeshPhysicalMaterial with PMREM env). Teaching framework layer is METAPHOR-LAYER-ONLY — VT/CRA/InteractionMode contracts (clm-pr-34) unchanged.
testCriteria: SpacekidEntity GLTF loads in < 3s on Chromebook-class hardware
AnimationMixer.crossFadeTo() transitions between idle/walk/board without visual pop
IBL + MeshPhysicalMaterial achieves photonicRealism visual target at 60fps stable on Chromebook
SkeletonUtils.clone() produces independent bone poses for 2+ simultaneous spacekid instances
MeshSSSNodeMaterial on spacesuit material does not drop frame rate below 50fps
architectureImplications: SpacekidEntity requires separate AnimationMixer per SkeletonUtils.clone() instance
Teaching framework migration is purely metaphor layer — resonance-loop.ts → spaceship entity, pulse-shard → spacekid entity
PBR material setup requires PMREMGenerator call once at scene init, not per frame
n8ao halfRes mode is the Chromebook performance escape valve (clm-pr-26)
MeshSSSNodeMaterial is selective-only (forehead/cheek areas) to preserve perf budget

devcon5Alignment.DDD: SpacekidEntity/SpaceshipEntity vocabulary matches domain story terms. AnimationState maps VT/CRA beat transitions to animation clip selections.
devcon5Alignment.DRY: Single PMREMGenerator call at scene init. Single AnimationStateMachine drives all character instances. No per-entity lighting setup.
devcon5Alignment.OCP: New character models added by dropping new GLTF — MaterialApply ACTION unchanged. New animation states added to AnimationStateMachine without breaking existing crossfade logic.
devcon5Alignment.PECS: SpacekidConfig (producer DATA) extends interface; CharacterRenderSystem (consumer LOGIC) reads without requiring concrete class.

hypotheses.label: Procedural Geometry + MeshPhysicalMaterial Stack
hypotheses.researchQuestion: q-pr-01 / q-pr-02 — Rendering approach and character visual pipeline (competing hypothesis)
hypotheses.statement: This stack is optimal for this D/L/A structure because: procedural geometry (DATA) defines entities via mathematical parameters, eliminating external GLTF asset pipeline complexity; MeshPhysicalMaterial (LOGIC) derives PBR appearance from parameter structs with no animation rig overhead; Three.js built-in geometries (SphereGeometry, CylinderGeometry, TorusGeometry) compose to form spacekid characters with zero external dependencies; n8ao SSAO + IBL achieves photonicRealism aesthetic without skeletal animation. Simpler D/L/A surface: ProceduralCharacterSpec = DATA struct, MaterialDerivation = LOGIC DerivedProperty, GeometryBuild = ACTION type.
hypotheses.dlaJustification: DATA: ProceduralCharacterSpec (body radius, helmet color, visor transmission, suit roughness), ProceduralSpaceshipSpec (ring geometry params, material arrays). LOGIC: PhotonicMaterialDeriver (DATA params → MeshPhysicalMaterial instance), ProceduralRigAnimator (time-based procedural motion — bob, float, rotate). ACTION: GeometryAssembly (assemble Group from primitives), SceneAdd, MaterialApply. No external GLTF pipeline, no Mixamo/Blender dependency.
testCriteria: Procedural spacekid assembly achieves comparable photonicRealism visual target as GLTF character
Procedural float/bob animations are sufficiently expressive for educational engagement
ProceduralCharacterSpec parameter space enables meaningful visual differentiation between characters
No GLTF loader required — zero external asset dependencies at runtime
architectureImplications: No Mixamo/Blender/RPM pipeline — reduces asset production overhead but limits character expressiveness
SpaceshipEntity procedural geometry is already the correct approach per ontologist (clm-pr-35)
SpacekidEntity procedural approach sacrifices animation fidelity — educational engagement risk for character-based learning interactions
ProceduralRigAnimator requires bespoke implementation — no existing Three.js helper
Easier to scale entity count (no SkinnedMesh instancing complexity)

devcon5Alignment.DDD: ProceduralCharacterSpec uses domain terms (helmetColor, visortransmission) not raw geometry params.
devcon5Alignment.DRY: Single PhotonicMaterialDeriver creates all character materials from spec. Single ProceduralRigAnimator drives all procedural motion.
devcon5Alignment.OCP: New character variant added by extending ProceduralCharacterSpec with new params — existing GeometryAssembly ACTION unchanged.
devcon5Alignment.PECS: ProceduralCharacterSpec (producer DATA) extends interface; CharacterRenderSystem (consumer LOGIC) reads spec without concrete class binding.

hypotheses.label: Factory+Registry Component System
hypotheses.statement: This stack is optimal for this D/L/A structure because: Factory+Registry (LOGIC) enables OCP component extension for <50 entities without full ECS framework overhead; ComponentRegistry (DATA) stores createFn per component type; ComponentFactory (LOGIC) applies registered creators on demand; the D/L/A surface maps cleanly — ComponentSpec = DATA struct, ComponentFactory = LOGIC DerivedProperty, ComponentMount = ACTION type. This directly instantiates the ontologist recommendation (clm-pr-23): Factory+Registry is sufficient for mathcrew scale without miniplex/bitECS overhead.
hypotheses.dlaJustification: DATA: ComponentSpec (type, config params), ComponentRegistry (Map<ComponentType, CreateFn>). LOGIC: ComponentFactory (registry lookup + createFn dispatch), ComponentQueryResolver (entity-to-component graph traversal). ACTION: ComponentMount (createFn call + scene.add), ComponentUnmount (dispose + remove), ComponentUpdate (per-frame system update call). The registry IS the ontology surface — each registered component maps to exactly one D/L/A domain object.
testCriteria: New component type (e.g. ProximityDetector) registered with 0 changes to existing component code
ComponentFactory.create() handles SpacekidEntity + SpaceshipEntity + EnvironmentEntity types
Entity count stays under 50 — if > 50 entities required, hypothesis refuted (migrate to miniplex)
architectureImplications: Registry is the single source of truth for component types — no scattered factory functions
Each entity type (SpacekidEntity, SpaceshipEntity) has a corresponding registry entry
System functions (RenderSystem, AnimationSystem, InteractionSystem) operate over registry queries
Migration path to miniplex ECS is clean: replace registry Map with miniplex World, system functions become miniplex systems

devcon5Alignment.DDD: Registry component types match domain vocabulary (SpacekidMaterialComponent, AnimationStateComponent, ProximityTriggerComponent).
devcon5Alignment.DRY: Single registry eliminates per-entity factory duplication. ComponentFactory.create() is called once per component type.
devcon5Alignment.OCP: New component: one registry.register() call — no changes to existing factory, query, or mount logic.
devcon5Alignment.PECS: ComponentSpec (producer DATA) extends interface; ComponentFactory (consumer LOGIC) reads spec generically.

hypotheses.label: miniplex ECS Component System
hypotheses.statement: This stack is optimal for this D/L/A structure because: miniplex ECS (LOGIC) provides type-safe component composition using object identity (not numeric IDs), enabling idiomatic TypeScript entity management; the World object maps cleanly to the D/L/A surface — entities are plain DATA objects, systems are LOGIC functions over component queries, add/remove are ACTION calls; miniplex 6k+ stars and developer-friendly API reduces risk vs bitECS for educational game scale.
hypotheses.dlaJustification: DATA: Entity objects (plain JS objects with typed component fields — { mesh, material, animation, interaction }). LOGIC: miniplex World (query engine, archetype tracking), SystemFunctions (RenderSystem, AnimationSystem, InteractionSystem). ACTION: world.add(entity), world.remove(entity), component mutation via direct entity property assignment. This is cleaner D/L/A separation than Factory+Registry — entities ARE data, systems ARE logic.
testCriteria: miniplex World.with() queries return correct entity subsets for RenderSystem and AnimationSystem
Entity add/remove in miniplex does not leak Three.js resources (requires manual dispose in system)
miniplex bundle size addition is < 5KB gzipped (acceptable for mathcrew web target)
architectureImplications: miniplex adds external dependency (vs Factory+Registry zero dependencies)
World state is the authoritative entity registry — no parallel Map<> tracking needed
miniplex v2 API uses world.with(...components).entities — TypeScript-idiomatic
Requires explicit Three.js resource cleanup (geometry.dispose(), material.dispose()) in entity remove handler

devcon5Alignment.DDD: Entity component names match domain vocabulary (SpacekidMaterialComponent, AnimationStateComponent).
devcon5Alignment.DRY: World query replaces all Map<> tracking and per-system entity lists. Single world as source of truth.
devcon5Alignment.OCP: New component type: extend entity interface with new field — existing systems ignore unknown fields.
devcon5Alignment.PECS: Entity (producer DATA) extends interface with new components; SystemFunction (consumer LOGIC) reads world query results.

hypotheses.label: DragControls+Proxy Interaction Paradigm
hypotheses.researchQuestion: q-pr-04 — Interaction paradigm for spacekid/spaceship placement
hypotheses.statement: This stack is optimal for this D/L/A structure because: DragControls (ACTION) is the official Three.js built-in drag solution; proxy mesh pattern (clm-pr-30) resolves GLTF+AnimationMixer incompatibility with DragControls; grid snap formula (clm-pr-31) implements discrete placement for math manipulative gameplay; proximity triggers (clm-pr-32) via Vector3.distanceTo() implement spatial relationship detection for game logic. D/L/A surface: DragState = DATA, SnapGrid = LOGIC, DragDispatch = ACTION.
hypotheses.dlaJustification: DATA: DragState (isDragging: boolean, dragTarget: EntityRef, snapPosition: Vector3), SnapGridConfig (cellSize, worldBounds). LOGIC: SnapPositionDeriver (dragPos → snapped Vector3), ProximityEvaluator (Vector3.distanceTo per entity pair → boolean). ACTION: DragStart (proxy bind), DragMove (position copy + snap apply), DragEnd (final position commit + InteractionMode trigger), ProximityEventDispatch. The proxy pattern cleanly separates DragControls binding (ACTION surface) from character Group positioning (LOGIC derive).
testCriteria: DragControls proxy pattern correctly positions animated GLTF spacekid Group without animation disruption
Grid snap formula produces discrete placement cells matching InteractionMode board positions
Vector3.distanceTo() proximity detection fires correctly when spacekid reaches spaceship threshold
touch-action:none prevents scroll conflicts on tablet (clm-pr-33)
architectureImplications: Proxy mesh must match character Group bounding box for accurate click/drag targeting
SnapGrid CONFIG is DATA — it is game-design-specified, not computed per-frame
Proximity detection is per-frame O(n) — acceptable for < 50 entities, use Octree if > 50
DragEnd triggers InteractionMode state transition — ACTION → teaching framework integration point

devcon5Alignment.DDD: DragState uses domain terms (dragTarget=EntityRef, not mesh ref). SnapGrid uses game design vocabulary (cellSize, not gridUnit).
devcon5Alignment.DRY: Single DragDispatch ACTION handles all entity types via EntityRef. Single ProximityEvaluator for all pair checks.
devcon5Alignment.OCP: New drag-enabled entity type: register proxy mesh — DragDispatch ACTION unchanged. New proximity threshold: update SnapGridConfig DATA — ProximityEvaluator LOGIC unchanged.
devcon5Alignment.PECS: DragState (producer DATA) extends interface; DragDispatch (consumer ACTION) reads target reference generically.

hypotheses.label: Gesture+Spatial Proximity Interaction Paradigm
hypotheses.statement: This stack is optimal for this D/L/A structure because: gesture recognition (pointer events + velocity analysis) provides more natural tablet interaction than explicit drag; spatial proximity triggers (Vector3.distanceTo) replace snap-to-grid with organic placement zones; this eliminates discrete grid dependency and supports freer entity placement for open-ended math exploration scenarios.
hypotheses.dlaJustification: DATA: GestureState (startPos, currentVelocity, gestureType: 'drag'|'flick'|'tap'), PlacementZone (center: Vector3, radius: number, acceptsEntityType: EntityType[]). LOGIC: GestureClassifier (pointer event stream → GestureType), PlacementZoneEvaluator (entity position → nearest zone). ACTION: GestureEventDispatch (classified gesture → InteractionMode trigger), ZonePlacement (snap to zone center if within radius).
testCriteria: GestureClassifier correctly distinguishes drag vs flick gestures on tablet
PlacementZone evaluation replaces DragControls proxy pattern without animation disruption
Gesture-based interaction achieves same InteractionMode completion rates as DragControls approach
architectureImplications: Gesture recognition requires custom implementation — no Three.js built-in (increases implementation complexity)
Spatial proximity zones require zone definition as DATA (design-time decision)
OS edge gesture conflicts (clm-pr-33 §GEST.OS-02) are higher risk without DragControls boundary controls
Grid snap is explicitly rejected — this changes math manipulative placement semantics (risk for structured math games)

devcon5Alignment.DDD: GestureState uses interaction domain vocabulary (flick, drag, tap) not raw pointer coordinates.
devcon5Alignment.DRY: Single GestureClassifier; single PlacementZoneEvaluator — no per-entity gesture logic.
devcon5Alignment.OCP: New gesture type: add GestureType variant — GestureClassifier logic open-closed.
devcon5Alignment.PECS: GestureState (producer DATA) extends interface; GestureEventDispatch (consumer ACTION) reads gesture type generically.

scenarios.label: H-1 Base: GLTF Pipeline + IBL + AnimationMixer Integration
scenarios.description: Standard GLTF pipeline (Kenney CC0 character → Blender rigging → Mixamo animation → GLB → gltfjsx compression) delivers photonicRealism spacekid with AnimationMixer crossfade and IBL via PMREMGenerator. MeshPhysicalMaterial for suit body, selective MeshSSSNodeMaterial for face/visor transmission. n8ao halfRes for Chromebook 60fps budget.
assumptions: 1. GLTF file is prepared via Blender+Mixamo pipeline (clm-pr-12) with matching bone names
2. SkeletonUtils.clone() correctly produces independent AnimationMixer instances per spacekid (clm-pr-04)
3. PMREMGenerator setup at scene init (not per-frame) keeps IBL cost near-zero at runtime (clm-pr-10, clm-pr-16)
4. n8ao halfRes reduces SSAO cost sufficiently for 60fps on Chromebook (clm-pr-26)
5. Teaching framework migration is metaphor-layer-only — 10 InteractionModes preserve existing contracts (clm-pr-34)

contradictions.claim: SkeletonUtils.retarget() reliability (clm-pr-15) — community reports inverted feet/backward hands with different-proportion skeletons
contradictions.resolution: Bundle animations directly in GLB (Mixamo FBX → Blender → GLB with embedded clips) — avoid runtime retarget() call. Use GLB with pre-baked Mixamo animations.
contradictions.resolutionEvidence: clm-pr-15 specifically flags retarget(), not pre-baked animation clip playback. GLB with embedded clips avoids retarget() entirely.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: clm-pr-04, clm-pr-09, clm-pr-10 are Official tier-1 confirmed APIs. IBL@60fps (clm-pr-17) is Synthesis tier-2. Minor gap: Chromebook-specific benchmark not available.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: GLTF pipeline requires Blender+Mixamo workflow (moderate tooling ramp). GLTFLoader+AnimationMixer is documented. 2-3 weeks estimated.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Risk: GLTF asset pipeline dependency (Blender/Mixamo required). Mitigation: Kenney CC0 assets available, pipeline is standard.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Can swap GLTF model without changing AnimationMixer logic. Material overrides are per-entity DATA — reversible.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: GLTF pipeline + IBL setup is 2-3 weeks to working demo. Teaching integration (metaphor layer only) is additional 1-2 weeks.
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: CC0 assets (Kenney, Polyhaven HDRIs) — no licensing concerns. Mixamo requires free Adobe account — acceptable.
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Three.js r183, GLTFLoader, AnimationMixer — all production-stable APIs with 5+ year history.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: SpacekidEntity (DATA), AnimationStateMachine (LOGIC), CharacterSpawn (ACTION) — clean boundaries. All 5 domains represented (no SECURITY needed, LEARN via ContentEngagement).
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Chain: SpacekidConfig (DATA) → CharacterRenderSystem (LOGIC) → CharacterSpawn (ACTION) → Three.js scene. Gap: AnimationStateMachine not yet designed as typed LOGIC object.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: AnimationState transitions can be agent-driven via typed ACTION calls. Missing: formal action schemas for agent discovery.
evaluationRationale.prototypeValidation: Not yet tested — score 3 (not yet tested, neutral pending prototype T7/T8).

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: SpacekidConfig + SpaceshipConfig structs, PBR material params as DATA — define types in src/types/entities.ts
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: AnimationStateMachine LOGIC + CharacterSpawn ACTION + DragDispatch ACTION — operational entity system
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent-composable CharacterSpawn with typed EntitySpec — agents can spawn/configure entities via ontology ACTION calls

scenarios.label: H-1 Best: Kenney CC0 + One-Day GLTF Setup + Polyhaven HDRI
scenarios.description: Optimal conditions: Kenney Space Kit provides ready-to-use CC0 GLTF spacekid character with basic rig. Polyhaven 1K HDRI loads in < 200ms. AnimationMixer.crossFadeTo() transitions work without pop on first prototype. n8ao halfRes maintains 60fps on both Chromebook and tablet. MeshSSSNodeMaterial selective application (forehead only) has negligible performance cost.
assumptions: 1. Kenney Space Kit character is Mixamo-compatible without custom rigging (clm-pr-13)
2. MeshSSSNodeMaterial on < 10% of character surface area has < 2ms additional fragment shader cost
3. Polyhaven HDRI at 1K resolution (< 2MB) achieves acceptable IBL quality for educational context
4. crossFadeTo() transitions look correct on first implementation attempt without custom tuning
5. gltfjsx -S -T reduces Kenney character GLB to < 500KB (clm-pr-14)

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Kenney Space Kit GLTF compatibility with Mixamo is assumed (clm-pr-13 says CC0 assets available, not specifically Mixamo-compatible). Gap: requires verification in prototype.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: If CC0 character is pre-rigged for Mixamo, GLTF setup reduces to GLTFLoader + PMREMGenerator — 1-2 days.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Best case: zero licensing risk, zero perf issues. Negligible risk if Kenney character works directly.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Fully reversible — swap Kenney character for custom asset, same AnimationMixer code.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Days — quick prototype achievable with Kenney + Polyhaven HDRI.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Same as base — clean D/L/A boundaries.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Chain valid — Kenney asset → GLTFLoader DATA → AnimationMixer LOGIC → scene ACTION.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Same limitation as base — agent schemas not yet formal.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Download Kenney Space Kit → verify Mixamo compat → define SpacekidConfig struct
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: IBL setup + AnimationMixer + DragControls proxy pattern — all in Phase II
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Entity spawn via agent-callable ACTION spec — Phase III

scenarios.label: H-1 Worst: GLTF Pipeline Blocking Issues
scenarios.description: Worst case: Kenney character rig is incompatible with Mixamo retargeting (bone naming mismatch), requiring custom Blender rigging. MeshSSSNodeMaterial drops Chromebook framerate below 40fps. SkeletonUtils.clone() has undocumented issues with r183 that cause bone sharing artifacts. HDRI loading blocks main thread causing first-frame stutter.
assumptions: 1. Kenney Space Kit character requires significant Blender work to achieve Mixamo compatibility (falsifies clm-pr-13)
2. MeshSSSNodeMaterial has high fragment shader cost even at selective application
3. SkeletonUtils.clone() behavior on r183 has regressions from documented pattern
4. HDRI at 1K blocks main thread on low-end Chromebook

contradictions.claim: SkeletonUtils.retarget() issues (clm-pr-15) may indicate broader skeleton system instability in r183
contradictions.resolution: clm-pr-15 is specifically about retarget() (different skeleton proportions), not clone(). clone() is a well-tested pattern per clm-pr-04. This is an irreconcilable concern until prototype test.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Worst-case assumptions are speculative — evidence supports the opposite (clm-pr-04 confirms clone() works). Score 2 = speculative scenario.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: If GLTF rig requires custom Blender work: 1-2 months. Blocks Phase II.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Severe: custom rig work delays prototype significantly. MeshSSSNodeMaterial perf issue would require architecture redesign.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Can fall back to H-2 (procedural geometry) if GLTF pipeline fails — weeks of rework but not catastrophic.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 months if Blender custom rigging required.
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: No compliance issues in worst case.
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Three.js is still mature — ecosystem concern not the failure mode here.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: D/L/A design remains valid even if implementation is harder.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: HDRI load blocking would break Phase I golden tables path.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Worse if clone() issues prevent reliable entity spawning.
evaluationRationale.prototypeValidation: Not yet tested — these are hypothesis failure modes to verify in T7.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: BLOCKED if Blender rigging required — Kenney compatibility must be verified first
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Delayed by 4-8 weeks in worst case
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Cannot reach Phase III until Phase II operational

scenarios.label: H-1 Adversarial: Asset Pipeline Complexity Creep
scenarios.description: Adversarial scenario: GLTF pipeline (RPM → Blender → Mixamo → GLB → gltfjsx) introduces scope creep. Each step adds a tool dependency. RPM requires API key for advanced features. Mixamo requires Adobe account. Blender version mismatch causes export issues. gltfjsx CLI has compatibility issues with r183. The educational team ends up maintaining 5 separate tool setups instead of coding.
assumptions: 1. RPM API key management introduces CI/CD complexity (clm-pr-11 requires RPM account)
2. Mixamo account dependency creates bus-factor risk (one person's Adobe account)
3. Blender version pinning required for reproducible GLB exports
4. gltfjsx CLI output requires manual TypeScript adjustments per r183 API changes

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Tool dependency risk is real but mitigatable. Kenney CC0 (clm-pr-13) avoids RPM/Mixamo complexity for initial prototype.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Pipeline complexity overhead: 1-2 months if full RPM+Mixamo+Blender pipeline required. Kenney shortcut reduces to days.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Moderate: tool complexity is manageable with documented pipeline. Not project-fatal.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mitigation: use Kenney CC0 assets directly. Fallback to H-2 procedural if GLTF pipeline blocks.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Full RPM+Mixamo pipeline: 1-2 months before prototype. Kenney shortcut: days.
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: Mixamo free account is acceptable. RPM commercial terms require review for educational product.
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Individual tools are mature but pipeline integration is fragile.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Pipeline complexity doesn't change D/L/A design quality.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Tool complexity doesn't break the ontology chain but delays implementation.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agent-driven pipeline management would require additional tooling.

nextExperiment.description: Verify: can Kenney Space Kit character be used directly with Three.js AnimationMixer using pre-included idle/walk animations (no Mixamo required)? If yes, adversarial scenario risk is largely mitigated.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Start with Kenney CC0 — validate GLTF compat BEFORE committing to full RPM pipeline
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Only adopt full pipeline after Kenney validation proves insufficient
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Pipeline automation reduces risk if CI/CD bakes asset steps

scenarios.label: H-2 Base: Procedural Geometry + IBL + ProceduralRigAnimator
scenarios.description: Procedural spacekid assembled from Three.js primitives (SphereGeometry for helmet, CylinderGeometry for body, TorusGeometry for ring details). MeshPhysicalMaterial applied per-part with IBL. ProceduralRigAnimator provides time-based float/bob/rotate motion. SpaceshipEntity uses procedural geometry (already the correct design per ontologist). No GLTF loader, no Blender/Mixamo dependency.
assumptions: 1. Procedural geometry can achieve sufficient visual appeal for educational engagement in spacecraft context
2. ProceduralRigAnimator time-based motion is sufficient for character expressiveness
3. MeshPhysicalMaterial + IBL achieves photonicRealism without skeletal animation
4. Zero external GLTF asset dependency reduces implementation complexity significantly (clm-pr-23)

contradictions.claim: Procedural geometry for spacekid lacks the expressive character required for pedagogically engaging character-based learning interactions
contradictions.resolution: This is irreconcilable without user study evidence. Pedagogical engagement with procedural characters vs rigged characters is an open research question. Cannot be resolved with technical evidence alone.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Procedural approach is supported by clm-pr-35 (SpaceshipEntity is procedural — same pattern). Missing: evidence that procedural character achieves educational engagement target.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Simpler than GLTF pipeline — no external tools. ProceduralRigAnimator is bespoke but straightforward. 1-2 weeks.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Moderate: pedagogical engagement risk if procedural character is insufficiently expressive.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Fully reversible — swap procedural character for GLTF at any time. Materials/IBL code unchanged.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Days to working procedural spacekid with IBL. Fast prototype.
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: Zero external asset dependencies — fully compliant.
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Procedural Three.js geometry is foundational, mature API.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: ProceduralCharacterSpec (DATA), PhotonicMaterialDeriver (LOGIC), GeometryAssembly (ACTION) — clean boundaries.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Chain: ProceduralCharacterSpec → MaterialDeriver → GeometryAssembly → scene. Clean forward propagation.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: ProceduralCharacterSpec is a plain DATA struct — highly agent-composable. Agent can specify any visual variant via typed params.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: ProceduralCharacterSpec struct definition — fast path, data-first
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: PhotonicMaterialDeriver LOGIC + GeometryAssembly ACTION — operational in Phase II
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent-parameterizable characters via typed ProceduralCharacterSpec — strong Phase III composability

scenarios.label: H-2 Best: Procedural Geometry Fast Path to Photonicrealism
scenarios.description: Best case: procedural geometry + IBL achieves photonicRealism target in 1-2 days. ProceduralRigAnimator float/bob motion is visually appealing in educational context. Zero external tool dependencies. Teaching framework integration (metaphor layer only) takes 1 additional day. Full working prototype before H-1 GLTF pipeline is even set up.
assumptions: 1. Procedural spacekid is visually sufficient for planned math interactions (clm-pr-35)
2. IBL + PBR achieves photonicRealism for round/cylinder geometries (confirmed by clm-pr-16, clm-pr-17)
3. ProceduralRigAnimator implementation takes < 1 day (straightforward sin/cos time animation)

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Partial: SpaceshipEntity procedural approach is confirmed correct (clm-pr-35). SpacekidEntity procedural approach is inferred — not explicitly validated.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Trivial — built-in geometries, 1 day to working prototype.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Negligible — pure Three.js primitives, no external dependencies.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Fully reversible.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Days — fastest path to working visual.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Clean D/L/A mapping.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Healthy chain.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Strong — pure DATA spec drives everything.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Immediate — define ProceduralCharacterSpec, implement GeometryAssembly in Phase I
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Add ProceduralRigAnimator (LOGIC) + interaction triggers (ACTION) — Phase II
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent creates character variants via ProceduralCharacterSpec params — Phase III

scenarios.label: H-2 Worst: Procedural Characters Fail Engagement Benchmark
scenarios.description: Worst case: procedural spacekid geometry achieves photonicRealism aesthetic but fails educational engagement benchmark. Students disengage with non-expressive characters. Teaching framework interaction modes that depend on character facial expression or gesture cannot be implemented. Decision-log requirement req-pr-01 (entity visual transformation) not achievable with static procedural geometry.
assumptions: 1. Educational engagement requires expressive character animation (not proven by current evidence)
2. 10 InteractionMode types include character-specific interactions that fail with procedural geometry
3. Pedagogical effectiveness of procedural vs rigged characters is negative for target age group (Korean elementary school)

contradictions.claim: Current codebase uses abstract shapes (pulse-shards = rings) for math manipulatives — educational effectiveness is already proven without expressive characters
contradictions.resolution: This argues AGAINST the worst case. The current codebase shows abstract geometry is acceptable for math objects (rings = pulse-shards). However spacekid is a teaching CHARACTER, not a math manipulative — different pedagogical role. Evidence gap: no data on character expressiveness requirement for Korean elementary math.
contradictions.resolutionEvidence: clm-pr-35 confirms current codebase uses abstract shapes successfully. Different role (character vs manipulative) remains an unresolved design question but not a contradiction.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: evidenceSufficiency insufficient — worst case assumption about character expressiveness requirement has no evidence base.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Implementation is easy (procedural geometry), failure would be discovered post-implementation.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Severe if character engagement fails — would require migration to H-1 GLTF pipeline mid-project.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — can add GLTF character on top of procedural geometry code.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Fast to implement, but engagement failure discovered late.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: D/L/A design remains valid. Risk is pedagogical not structural.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Chain valid — risk is in outcome, not propagation.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: No impact.

nextExperiment.description: Conduct informal usability test: show Korean elementary school student both procedural spacekid and GLTF spacekid. Measure engagement preference. This resolves the pedagogical expressiveness gap.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Fast path — ProceduralCharacterSpec defined immediately
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Engagement failure risk manifests in Phase II user testing
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Phase III blocked if Phase II engagement benchmark fails

scenarios.label: H-2 Adversarial: Procedural Geometry Visual Parity Gap
scenarios.description: Adversarial: procedural geometry achieves photonicRealism for SpaceshipEntity (as planned) but fails visual parity for SpacekidEntity. The character requires fine-grained detail (fingers for pointing, face for expression) that procedural primitive geometry cannot provide. IBL highlights look beautiful on smooth sphere-helmet but artificial on combined cylinder-body geometry.
assumptions: 1. MeshPhysicalMaterial + IBL looks good on SphereGeometry (confirmed: clm-pr-17)
2. Multi-part combined geometry (5-10 primitives) has uncanny valley effect for character representation
3. SpacekidEntity character detail requirement exceeds what procedural primitives can express

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Adversarial assumption not supported by evidence — no data on multi-part primitive visual quality for educational characters.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Visual concern is discovered fast in prototype — no significant implementation cost before failure.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Moderate: aesthetic concern. Mitigation: use H-1 GLTF for SpacekidEntity, keep H-2 procedural for SpaceshipEntity.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Fully reversible — replace procedural SpacekidEntity with GLTF variant, procedural SpaceshipEntity unchanged.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Fast to prototype and evaluate.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: D/L/A design supports hybrid (GLTF SpacekidEntity + procedural SpaceshipEntity).
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Hybrid approach valid — different rendering paths per entity type.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: No impact.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Prototype both procedural SpacekidEntity and SpaceshipEntity — compare visual fidelity
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Hybrid decision: procedural SpaceshipEntity (confirmed), GLTF SpacekidEntity if procedural fails
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent can switch entity rendering path via EntitySpec DATA field

scenarios.label: H-3 Base: Factory+Registry Standard Operation
scenarios.description: ComponentRegistry Map<ComponentType, CreateFn> manages all entity types (SpacekidEntity, SpaceshipEntity, EnvironmentEntity, DragProxyEntity). ComponentFactory.create() dispatches to registered creators. Systems (RenderSystem, AnimationSystem, InteractionSystem) query registry and iterate over registered entities. All entity logic routes through typed registry interface.
assumptions: 1. Entity count stays under 50 throughout mathcrew development (clm-pr-23)
2. ComponentRegistry Map<> lookup is O(1) and has no performance overhead for < 50 entities
3. TypeScript Map<ComponentType, CreateFn> provides sufficient type safety without ECS framework
4. Teaching framework InteractionMode callbacks integrate cleanly with ComponentFactory event system

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: clm-pr-23 is Inference tier (confidence 0.75) — Factory+Registry adequacy for mathcrew scale is inferred, not benchmarked.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Trivial — Map<> + factory functions. No external dependencies. < 1 week.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Minor — if entity count exceeds 50, migration to miniplex is clean and low-risk.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Fully reversible — replace Map<> with miniplex World, factory functions become systems.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Days — no setup required beyond TypeScript Map<>.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: ComponentSpec (DATA), ComponentFactory (LOGIC DerivedProperty), ComponentMount (ACTION) — clean boundaries.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Chain: ComponentSpec (DATA) → ComponentFactory.create() (LOGIC) → ComponentMount (ACTION) → scene. Clean.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Factory can be called by agent via typed ComponentSpec. Missing: formal schema for agent discovery.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Define ComponentSpec types for all entity types — data-first registry
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Register CreateFn per component type — operational factory
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Expose ComponentSpec as agent-readable ontology action schema

scenarios.label: H-3 Best: Zero-Dependency Entity Management
scenarios.description: Best case: Factory+Registry implemented in < 2 hours using TypeScript Map<> and discriminated union ComponentType. All entity types registered on init. ComponentFactory.create() handles SpacekidEntity + SpaceshipEntity + 3 environment types. Teaching framework integration requires zero changes to InteractionMode contracts.
assumptions: 1. Entity types are stable for Phase I — no new types required during initial implementation
2. ComponentType discriminated union provides sufficient type safety
3. InteractionMode callbacks are already callable without registry changes

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: clm-pr-23 confidence 0.75 (Inference) — adequate but not high-confidence evidence.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Trivial: 2-4 hours.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Negligible in best case.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Complete.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Hours.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Same as base.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Same as base.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Same limitation — agent schemas not yet formal.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Immediate — TypeScript Map<> is a Phase I data structure
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Register systems and factory functions — Phase II
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Expose registry as ontology surface — Phase III

scenarios.label: H-3 Worst: Entity Count Exceeds Registry Design Limit
scenarios.description: Worst case: entity count grows beyond 50 as teaching framework expands (fractional spacekids, multiple spaceship types per level, environment detail objects). Factory+Registry Map<> becomes a performance bottleneck. Missing change tracking (no reactive updates) causes rendering inconsistencies. InteractionMode state management requires per-entity tracking that the registry pattern doesn't natively support.
assumptions: 1. Level design requires > 50 simultaneous entities
2. InteractionMode state requires entity-level change detection not available in Map<> pattern
3. Factory+Registry has no reactive update mechanism — all updates are imperative

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Worst case assumption (>50 entities) contradicts clm-pr-23 which explicitly states <50 entities for mathcrew. Speculative.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Migration from Map<> to miniplex is documented and clean — moderate difficulty.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Moderate: performance degradation. Mitigation: migrate to miniplex (clean path).
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — miniplex migration is mechanical.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Fast to initial implementation — failure is discovered in Phase II.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Worst case reveals missing reactive update pattern in D/L/A design.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Imperative update model is a ForwardProp gap if reactive updates needed.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Without reactive updates, agent-driven state changes are harder to observe.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Define entity count budget in ComponentSpec DATA — make it explicit
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Monitor entity count in Phase II — trigger miniplex migration if > 40
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: AI-First requires reactive entity model — consider miniplex regardless of count

scenarios.label: H-3 Adversarial: Registry Becomes Parallel Truth Source
scenarios.description: Adversarial: developers bypass ComponentFactory.create() and instantiate entities directly (new THREE.Group() + manual add to scene). Registry becomes out of sync. Debugging is hard because some entities are registry-tracked and some are not. This is a classic OCP violation — the architecture is correct but developer discipline breaks the contract.
assumptions: 1. Development team creates entities outside ComponentFactory in time-pressed feature development
2. No enforcement mechanism prevents direct instantiation
3. Registry becomes stale — ComponentFactory.create() has ghost entries

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Process risk (developer bypass) is not addressed by technical evidence.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Architecture requires TypeScript enforcement to prevent bypass — consider sealed factory pattern.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Moderate: registry staleness causes debugging overhead.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Difficult: if bypass becomes widespread, refactoring all entity instantiation is weeks of work.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Instant — this scenario emerges gradually over development.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: D/L/A mapping breaks down if entities exist outside registry — domain objects disconnected from ontology.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: ForwardProp chain broken: entities without registry entries cannot participate in system updates.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agents cannot discover or operate on non-registry entities — agent composability fails.

nextExperiment.description: Add TypeScript-enforced factory pattern: entity types are opaque (factory returns readonly interface, not class). This makes direct Three.js instantiation type-incompatible with game loop entity consumers.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Enforce: ComponentSpec types are opaque interfaces. Factory is the only producer.
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Code review gate: reject PRs with direct THREE.Group() entity instantiation
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent composability requires registry — enforcement is prerequisite for Phase III

scenarios.label: H-4 Base: miniplex ECS Standard Operation
scenarios.description: miniplex World manages all entities with typed components. world.with(HasMesh, HasAnimation) queries return typed entity subsets for RenderSystem and AnimationSystem. Entity add/remove is reactive — world notifies registered systems. Teaching framework interaction callbacks operate on entity component data. Bundle size addition: ~3KB gzipped.
assumptions: 1. miniplex v3 API is compatible with TypeScript strict mode (clm-pr-21 confirms TS support)
2. miniplex bundle size < 5KB gzipped is acceptable for mathcrew web target
3. Three.js resource cleanup (geometry.dispose() on entity remove) can be handled in miniplex remove event
4. miniplex entity model aligns with D/L/A DATA domain (entities are plain objects)

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: clm-pr-21 confirms miniplex API and developer ergonomics. Missing: explicit TypeScript strict mode compatibility for r183 + Bun.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Straightforward — miniplex is developer-friendly. 1-2 weeks with Three.js integration.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Minor — well-maintained library, 6k stars.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — can extract systems back to factory pattern if needed.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 weeks to working ECS-integrated entity system.
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: MIT license — acceptable. Adds external dependency.
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: 2+ years in production use. Growing adoption. Not LTS.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: ECS is the cleanest D/L/A mapping: entities = DATA, systems = LOGIC, world mutations = ACTION. Score 5.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Reactive world notifications provide forward propagation for all system updates.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: world.with() queries are agent-addressable. Entity components are typed DATA accessible via ontology.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Define entity component interfaces — World entity = DATA struct
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Register systems + queries — miniplex World is the operational register
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent queries world via ontology-mapped component filters — strong Phase III

scenarios.label: H-4 Best: miniplex ECS Cleanest D/L/A Architecture
scenarios.description: Best case: miniplex provides the cleanest possible D/L/A separation — entities are pure DATA, systems are pure LOGIC, add/remove mutations are pure ACTION. World state is the single source of truth for all entities. Teaching framework integration is a miniplex system (TeachingSystem) that queries entities with InteractionState component.
assumptions: 1. miniplex World replaces all current scattered entity management code
2. TeachingSystem as miniplex system provides clean LOGIC separation from game loop
3. miniplex reactive queries eliminate need for manual entity tracking lists

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Same evidence as base. Best case assumes clean integration from start.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Straightforward — miniplex is developer-friendly by design.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Negligible in best case.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 weeks.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Perfect D/L/A separation.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Reactive world = full forward prop health.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: World.with() = agent-composable queries at full score.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Define all component interfaces — world = DATA
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Register all systems — LOGIC queries over world
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent = miniplex system — directly composable

scenarios.label: H-4 Worst: miniplex API Instability Blocks Integration
scenarios.description: Worst case: miniplex v3 has breaking API changes. World.with() signature changes between minor versions. TypeScript type inference fails with Three.js complex generic types. Three.js resource cleanup on entity remove is not obvious from miniplex API — leaks geometry/material.
assumptions: 1. miniplex < 3 years in production — API may change (clm-pr-21 notes 'aging' freshness)
2. Three.js Object3D type complexity causes miniplex type inference failures
3. Entity remove does not automatically call Three.js dispose() — developer must hook it

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: API instability risk is speculative — miniplex has 6k stars and appears stable.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: TypeScript integration debugging: moderate.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Moderate: resource leaks are real risk but solvable with dispose() hooks.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — can revert to Factory+Registry if miniplex API fails.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: API compatibility debugging adds 1-2 weeks.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: ECS D/L/A design remains valid regardless of API issues.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Reactive queries at risk if API changes — ForwardProp partially breaks.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agent query API at risk.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Pin miniplex version — add to package.json with exact version
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Test Three.js dispose() integration in Phase II prototype
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent composability depends on stable world.with() API

scenarios.label: H-4 Adversarial: ECS Over-Engineering for Educational Game Scale
scenarios.description: Adversarial: miniplex ECS architecture is correct for 10,000 entities (clm-pr-22 bitECS scale claim) but over-engineered for 20-50 entities in mathcrew. Developer spends time learning ECS patterns (archetypes, system scheduling, component composition) that provide zero value for an educational game with 20 spacekids. Factory+Registry (H-3) achieves same goals with 10x less conceptual overhead.
assumptions: 1. miniplex learning curve adds 1 week overhead vs Factory+Registry zero overhead
2. ECS archetype queries provide zero performance benefit for 20-50 entities
3. Team is unfamiliar with ECS pattern — onboarding cost is real

contradictions.claim: clm-pr-23 (Inference) says Factory+Registry is sufficient. clm-pr-21 (Official) says miniplex is developer-friendly for TypeScript. These are irreconcilable preferences — both are valid approaches for different teams.
contradictions.resolution: Irreconcilable: both H-3 and H-4 are valid architectural choices. The selection depends on team familiarity and future entity scale ambition. This is a project context decision, not a technical correctness decision.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Both sides of the tension have evidence. Score 3 = partial support.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: ECS learning curve is moderate but one-time cost.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Minor: over-engineering is a team velocity concern, not a technical failure.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible to Factory+Registry.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 weeks vs H-3's hours.
evaluationRationale.governanceCompliance: MIT license — external dep is minor concern.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: ECS remains the cleanest D/L/A model.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Healthy regardless of over-engineering concern.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Strong regardless.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Start with Factory+Registry — prove it insufficient before adopting ECS
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: If entity system grows complex in Phase II, migrate to miniplex
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Phase III AI-First favors miniplex world.with() agent queries

scenarios.label: H-5 Base: DragControls+Proxy Standard Operation
scenarios.description: DragControls bound to invisible BoxGeometry proxy meshes (one per draggable entity). Entity Group.position updated to proxy.position in animation loop. Grid snap applied via Math.round formula on 'drag' event. Proximity detection via Vector3.distanceTo per-frame. touch-action:none on canvas for tablet compatibility.
assumptions: 1. DragControls proxy pattern (clm-pr-30) works correctly with Three.js r183
2. Grid snap formula (clm-pr-31) produces integer-valued cell positions matching InteractionMode board
3. Vector3.distanceTo() at 60fps for 20-50 entities has negligible performance cost
4. touch-action:none (clm-pr-33) resolves all tablet scroll conflicts

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: clm-pr-06 is Official for DragControls. clm-pr-30 is Synthesis (confidence 0.85). clm-pr-31 is Synthesis. All current freshnessStatus.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Straightforward — documented proxy pattern. 1-2 weeks including grid snap + proximity.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Minor — proxy pattern is standard workaround. DragControls is built-in.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — proxy mesh easily removed or replaced.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 weeks to working drag interaction.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: DragState (DATA), SnapPositionDeriver (LOGIC), DragDispatch (ACTION) — clean boundaries.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: DragEnd ACTION → InteractionMode trigger → teaching state. Clean chain.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: DragDispatch ACTION can be agent-initiated. Missing: formal schema.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Define DragState + SnapGridConfig as typed DATA structs
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Implement DragControls proxy + SnapPositionDeriver + ProximityEvaluator — Phase II
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent triggers DragDispatch ACTION via typed EntityRef + targetPosition — Phase III

scenarios.label: H-5 Best: DragControls Proxy Resolves GLTF Incompatibility Cleanly
scenarios.description: Best case: proxy pattern works first attempt without animation disruption. Grid snap formula produces visually-correct placement on first commit. Proximity detection fires correctly for all 10 InteractionMode types. touch-action:none has zero unexpected side effects on target tablets.
assumptions: 1. DragControls r183 proxy pattern is identical to documented community pattern (clm-pr-30)
2. Grid snap formula works for all InteractionMode board sizes
3. All 10 InteractionMode types have compatible placement semantics with grid snap

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: SnapGridConfig struct + DragState struct defined in Phase I
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Full drag system in Phase II — all interaction modes
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent-composable drag via ACTION schema

scenarios.label: H-5 Worst: DragControls Proxy Breaks AnimationMixer
scenarios.description: Worst case: proxy pattern causes animation discontinuity — AnimationMixer loses track of root motion when character Group.position is set imperatively from proxy position. Touch events on tablet fire both scroll and drag simultaneously despite touch-action:none (OS-level edge gesture conflicts per clm-pr-33). Grid snap produces half-cell offset due to world coordinate vs screen coordinate mismatch.
assumptions: 1. Imperatively setting Group.position from proxy position conflicts with AnimationMixer root motion (clm-pr-09)
2. iOS Safari ignores touch-action:none in certain gesture contexts
3. Grid snap formula requires camera-specific coordinate transformation not documented in clm-pr-31

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Worst case assumptions are speculative — proxy pattern is documented community practice.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: If root motion conflict occurs: significant debugging and possibly custom AnimationMixer integration.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Severe: touch interaction failure on tablet would be user-facing.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Significant refactoring if proxy pattern fails — weeks.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Debugging root motion conflicts could take weeks.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: D/L/A design remains valid — implementation complexity is the issue.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: DragEnd → InteractionMode chain breaks if drag doesn't complete correctly.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agent drag fails if underlying mechanism is broken.
evaluationRationale.prototypeValidation: Not yet tested — T7 prototype should specifically test proxy+AnimationMixer interaction.

nextExperiment.description: Prototype specifically: DragControls proxy bound to animated GLTF spacekid. Verify animation continues uninterrupted during drag. Test on tablet (touch-action). This is the critical unknown for H-5.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Phase I: define DragState DATA struct — independent of implementation
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Phase II critical test: proxy pattern with animated GLTF
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Phase III requires working drag for interactive learning scenarios

scenarios.label: H-5 Adversarial: Drag-Based Interaction is Cognitively Suboptimal
scenarios.description: Adversarial: drag-and-drop interaction paradigm for math manipulatives may introduce extraneous cognitive load per CLT. Students attend to the drag mechanism (where to put the spacekid) rather than the mathematical concept (grouping, counting). CTML Coherence Principle: drag UI is "decorative" if the interaction doesn't serve the learning objective. H-6 spatial proximity may achieve same math interaction with less extraneous UI mechanics.
assumptions: 1. Drag-and-drop adds extraneous cognitive load for Korean elementary school learners
2. Current InteractionMode types (pickup-drop, array-builder) require explicit drag — not optional
3. CTML Coherence violation: drag handle affordances add visual noise without learning benefit

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: CTML concern is a legitimate pedagogical risk but not supported by specific evidence for this age group.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: DragControls proxy is straightforward regardless of pedagogical debate.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Moderate: pedagogical effectiveness risk.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Can layer H-6 spatial proximity on top of drag — orthogonal systems.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: No impact on implementation timeline.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Interaction pattern doesn't affect D/L/A quality.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: No impact.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: No impact.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: InteractionMode DATA includes interactionType field — drag vs proximity are both DATA-specified
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Implement drag AND proximity — make both InteractionMode options available
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent selects interaction paradigm per student's CLT profile

scenarios.label: H-6 Base: Gesture+Proximity Standard Operation
scenarios.description: GestureClassifier processes pointer event stream and classifies drag/flick/tap. PlacementZones defined as sphere volumes in 3D world space. Entity moved toward nearest PlacementZone on flick gesture. ProximityEvaluator determines which zone entity entered. Teaching framework receives zone-based placement events.
assumptions: 1. Pointer event velocity analysis is sufficient for reliable gesture classification on tablet
2. PlacementZone sphere volumes are sufficient for math manipulative placement scenarios
3. GestureClassifier custom implementation is achievable in < 2 weeks

contradictions.claim: H-5 uses DragControls built-in (Official, clm-pr-06). H-6 requires custom GestureClassifier (no Three.js built-in). This is an irreconcilable complexity difference.
contradictions.resolution: Irreconcilable: H-5 uses official built-in API; H-6 requires custom implementation. Both are valid for different design goals. Split into separate scenarios as designed.

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Limited evidence for gesture-first approach. clm-pr-33 covers pointer events; clm-pr-32 covers proximity. GestureClassifier has no evidence support.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Custom gesture recognition is significantly harder than DragControls proxy. 1-2 months for reliable cross-device gesture classifier.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Moderate: gesture classification accuracy on tablet is highly device-dependent.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — can fall back to H-5 DragControls, proximity detection preserved.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: 1-2 months — long development cycle.
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Gesture classification is not an established Three.js pattern — emerging approach.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: GestureState (DATA), GestureClassifier (LOGIC), GestureEventDispatch (ACTION) — clean design.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Chain: GestureState → GestureClassifier → ZonePlacement → InteractionMode. Design is valid but implementation risk is high.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: GestureEventDispatch ACTION can be agent-triggered. Limited utility for automated testing.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Define GestureState + PlacementZone structs — fast DATA layer
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Custom GestureClassifier implementation — high Phase II risk
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Agent-composable if GestureState is typed — but Phase III unlikely before Phase II stable

scenarios.label: H-6 Best: Natural Gesture Paradigm Eliminates Drag UI Overhead
scenarios.description: Best case: GestureClassifier correctly classifies all gesture types on first tablet test. PlacementZones naturally align with math board layout. No drag handle affordances needed — touch-to-move feels natural for Korean elementary school learners. CTML extraneous load is lower than DragControls approach.
assumptions: 1. Pointer event velocity thresholds for flick vs drag are universal across target tablets
2. PlacementZone radii match natural finger placement precision of target age group
3. Gesture-based interaction produces correct InteractionMode event sequences

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: Best case assumptions are largely speculative — no evidence for gesture universality across tablets or age-appropriate precision.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Even in best case: GestureClassifier requires custom implementation — not trivial.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Minor in best case.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Still 1-2 months minimum for reliable implementation.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: D/L/A design is clean.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Chain valid if implementation succeeds.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Moderate.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Define GestureState DATA with typed gesture vocabulary
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Implement in Phase II — but de-prioritized vs H-5 DragControls
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Consider in Phase III as enhancement over baseline DragControls

scenarios.label: H-6 Worst: GestureClassifier Unreliable on Target Devices
scenarios.description: Worst case: GestureClassifier has < 70% classification accuracy on Galaxy Tab A and Samsung Chromebook (target school hardware). False positives trigger incorrect InteractionMode transitions. OS edge swipe gestures (clm-pr-33) steal events from canvas even with touch-action:none. Students abandon tasks due to unresponsive interaction.
assumptions: 1. Pointer event timing is inconsistent across Galaxy Tab A, iPad, and Chromebook
2. OS-level edge gestures steal events that cannot be prevented with CSS touch-action
3. Classification accuracy < 70% is unacceptable for educational interaction

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: OS edge gesture risk (clm-pr-33 §GEST.OS-02) is Official — edge zone conflicts are documented.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Extremely hard: cross-device gesture reliability requires extensive device testing.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Critical: unresponsive interaction on school hardware = project failure for this use case.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Fallback to H-5 DragControls is available — weeks of rework.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: > 2 months with device testing cycles.
evaluationRationale.ecosystemMaturity: Not established pattern — high ecosystem risk.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Classification failure means GestureState is unreliable — DATA quality breaks.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: Forward chain breaks at GestureClassifier LOGIC if classification is unreliable.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agent operations fail if gesture classification is unreliable.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: Phase I: define GestureState with accuracy metric — monitor from start
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: Phase II gate: if accuracy < 80% on target devices, abandon H-6 for H-5
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: Cannot reach Phase III with unreliable gesture input

scenarios.label: H-6 Adversarial: Gesture Approach Rejects Grid Math Manipulatives
scenarios.description: Adversarial: H-6 rejects the grid-snap paradigm (clm-pr-31) in favor of zone-based placement. However, 6 of 10 InteractionMode types (array-builder, venn-diagram, observe-factor-table, pickup-drop, divisor-judge, resonance-trial-sequencer) require discrete grid positions for mathematical correctness. Zone placement introduces positional ambiguity that is pedagogically invalid for discrete math manipulatives.
assumptions: 1. 6/10 InteractionModes require discrete (integer-valued) placement positions
2. PlacementZones cannot guarantee discrete placement precision
3. Mathematical correctness requires exact cell placement, not approximate zone membership

contradictions.claim: clm-pr-31 uses grid snap (discrete placement). clm-pr-36 confirms InteractionModes use array/venn/table structures requiring precise placement. H-6 PlacementZone rejects grid snap.
contradictions.resolution: Irreconcilable: H-6 must either incorporate grid snap within zones (defeating the gesture purpose) or accept mathematical ambiguity. This is a fundamental design tension between natural gesture (continuous) and math manipulative semantics (discrete).

evaluationRationale.evidenceFit: This contradiction is well-evidenced: clm-pr-31 (grid snap for math) + clm-pr-36 (InteractionMode array/grid types) both support the adversarial claim.
evaluationRationale.implementationDifficulty: Cannot resolve without fundamental redesign of gesture or interaction paradigm.
evaluationRationale.riskSeverity: Critical: mathematical incorrectness in educational tool is a fundamental failure.
evaluationRationale.reversibility: Mostly reversible — fall back to H-5 DragControls with grid snap.
evaluationRationale.timeToValue: Long development cycle, fundamental design conflict.
evaluationRationale.dlaFit: Adversarial scenario reveals D/L/A gap: InteractionMode DATA requires discrete placement, but H-6 LOGIC provides continuous zones.
evaluationRationale.forwardPropHealth: ForwardProp breaks: discrete InteractionMode DATA → continuous zone LOGIC → ambiguous ACTION. Chain semantics mismatch.
evaluationRationale.agentComposability: Agent cannot determine correct grid position from zone membership alone.

implementationJourney.phase1_golden_tables: This scenario invalidates H-6 as primary interaction paradigm for grid-based InteractionModes
implementationJourney.phase2_operational: H-6 may be valid for non-grid InteractionModes (observe, predict-verify) — hybrid approach possible
implementationJourney.phase3_ai_first: H-6 is Phase III enhancement for non-discrete interactions; H-5 remains primary

changesApplied: Generated all 6 hypotheses (H-1 through H-6) grounded in D/L/A ontology
Generated all 24 scenarios (4 per hypothesis) with initial scores
Classified all contradictions per scoring-rubric.md rules
revisionHistory.notes: Initial generation round. All scenarios created with complete 11-dimension scoring.

changesApplied: Verified all scenarios have >= 1 evidence ID — confirmed, all have evidenceBaseIds

Re-classified contradictions: S-H1-WORST CON-H1-WORST-01 marked unresolvable (requires prototype test)
Upgraded S-H3-BEST evidenceSufficiency to 'sufficient' (sufficient evidence for base factory pattern claim)
Added evaluationRationale to all scenarios for scoring transparency
Verified DevCon 5 alignment scores are present for all scenarios
Confirmed teaching framework layer-preservation (clm-pr-34) is accounted in H-1 and H-2
S-H6-ADVERSARIAL irreconcilable contradiction documented (grid math vs gesture continuous)
revisionHistory.notes: Revision round 2 — stopping criteria verification pass. All criteria met: no detected contradictions, no insufficient-evidence scenarios without NextExperiment, all 4 types per hypothesis, all 11 dimensions scored.

evidenceGaps.description: MeshSSSNodeMaterial FPS on Chromebook unverified. Subsurface scattering fragment shader cost at selective application (face/forehead area) — unknown whether it stays within 60fps budget on Chromebook-class GPU.

SkeletonUtils.clone() x5 draw call budget unverified. With 5 simultaneous spacekid instances (each via SkeletonUtils.clone()), draw call count and per-frame AnimationMixer update cost not benchmarked on Chromebook.

gltfjsx + Blender bake pipeline unverified. Mixamo FBX animation retargeting to custom spacekid rig via Blender — bone proportion mismatch risk (clm-pr-15). Pipeline reproducibility (Blender version pinning, gltfjsx CLI r183 compat) not validated.

DragControls proxy + AnimationMixer jitter unverified. Imperatively setting character Group.position from proxy mesh position per-frame may cause animation root-motion jitter when AnimationMixer crossFadeTo() is active. Simulator-identified gap — not in ontologist canonical registry.
Procedural spacekid pedagogical engagement vs GLTF rigged character for Korean elementary school. No user study evidence. Simulator-identified gap not in ontologist canonical registry.

winningHypotheses.rendering: H-1 preferred (GLTF+AnimationMixer+IBL). H-2 is valid fallback for SpaceshipEntity procedural (already planned per ontologist). Hybrid: GLTF SpacekidEntity + Procedural SpaceshipEntity.
winningHypotheses.componentSystem: H-3 preferred (Factory+Registry zero dependencies, maps to ontologist PhotonicComponentFactory object). H-4 is valid upgrade path if entity count exceeds 50 or Phase III AI-First requires reactive world queries.
winningHypotheses.interaction: H-5 preferred (DragControls+Proxy — maps to ontologist CharacterDragPlacement ACTION object). H-6 maps to GestureInteractionAdapter ACTION object but is disqualified as primary for grid-based math manipulatives per S-H6-ADVERSARIAL irreconcilable contradiction.

criticalPrototypeTests: g-pr-01 (HIGH): MeshSSSNodeMaterial FPS on Chromebook — T7 must benchmark selective SSS cost
g-pr-02 (HIGH): SkeletonUtils.clone() x5 draw call budget on Chromebook — T7 must benchmark
g-pr-03 (MEDIUM): gltfjsx + Blender bake pipeline — T7 must validate Mixamo→GLB workflow reproducibility
g-pr-sim-02 (MEDIUM): DragControls proxy + AnimationMixer jitter — T7 must verify proxy pattern with crossFadeTo() active
decisionSummary.teachingFrameworkNote: ALL hypotheses preserve VT/CRA/beats/ts-fsrs/InteractionMode contracts per clm-pr-34. Migration is metaphor-layer-only per clm-pr-35. TeachingStoryFrame (DATA) and TeachingFrameworkMigration (LOGIC) are ontologist-confirmed objects.
